title: Oleoresin Capsicum Spray (OC Decontamination)
content_markdown:
#### Indications

- The management of physiological effects of exposure to OC spray. 

#### Decontamination Procedure

- Ensure that you don’t become contaminated. Stand in an upwind position and wear PPE appropriate to the scene (i.e. gloves, eye protection and masks if required).

- Reassure patient that their symptoms will disappear during decontamination.

- Remove contaminated clothing to limit further exposure to the spray.

- Tell the patient not to rub their face, eyes or skin, as this will increase any pain that is being experienced.

- Expose the patient to fresh air where a breeze is present, preferably. A fan can also be used with good effect.

- Flush the affected area with copious amounts of cool water. A tap or hose can be utilised if available, otherwise a giving set and saline will suffice. If spray is dried in the hair, take care not to re-wet the hair, as its runoff will re-contaminate the face.

- Washing the face and eyelids with a mild oil free soap will assist in breaking down the oily consistency of the OC resin, thus speeding up its removal from the skin.

- Assist the patient in removing contact lenses as spray residue may accumulate under the lenses and continue irritating the eye.

- Continue to flush the eyes and concentrate on irrigating the eye under the lids.

#### Specific Notes

- Physiological effects may include: 
- **Eyes** - pain, burning, stinging, inflammation, tearing, eyelid spasm.
- **Respiratory** - swelling, bronchospasm, coughing, gagging, gasping, dysphasia. Respiratory function usually resolves in 10-45 minutes. Rarely, cyanosis and respiratory arrest can occur.
- **Skin** - swelling, burning, tingling, redness, blistering.
